description:
You are a monitoring specialist who needs to put yesterday’s alert logs into long–term storage and then verify that the archive can be restored.

  Starting point:
  • A directory called /home/user/alerts already exists.  
  • Inside /home/user/alerts there are exactly three log files:
      – cpu.log  
      – memory.log  
      – disk.log  

  Goal (what must be true when you are done):
  1. Create a compressed tarball named /home/user/alerts_archive.tar.gz that contains the three log files (and only those three files) from /home/user/alerts at the top level of the archive.
  2. Extract that archive into a new directory named /home/user/alerts_restore.  
     • The directory must contain cpu.log, memory.log and disk.log with byte-for-byte identical contents to the originals.
  3. Inside /home/user/alerts_restore generate a text file called restore_summary.log.  
     • The file must contain one line per extracted log file.  
     • Each line must follow the exact format:  
          <filename>:<size_in_bytes>  
     • Lines must be sorted alphabetically by <filename>.  
     • A Unix newline “\n” must terminate every line, including the last one.  
     • For the provided sample data the file should therefore read exactly:
          cpu.log:14
          disk.log:10
          memory.log:8
        (note the newline after “memory.log:8”).

  4. Leave the original /home/user/alerts directory and its contents untouched.

  Verifications the automated test will perform:
  • /home/user/alerts_archive.tar.gz exists and is a valid gzip-compressed tar archive.  
  • Listing the archive shows only cpu.log, memory.log and disk.log at the archive root.  
  • /home/user/alerts_restore exists and contains cpu.log, memory.log, disk.log and restore_summary.log.  
  • Checksums of the restored log files match the originals.  
  • restore_summary.log exists and its content is byte-for-byte exactly as specified above.

  Hint: This can be achieved with two or three common command-line utilities (tar, gzip, etc.).
truth:
Initial filesystem state before the agent starts:
  └── /home/user/alerts
      ├── cpu.log        (14 bytes, content = "CPU: all good\n")
      ├── memory.log     (8 bytes,  content = "MEM: ok\n")
      └── disk.log       (10 bytes, content = "DISK: 70%\n")

  Exact file contents:
  1. /home/user/alerts/cpu.log
     CPU: all good\n
     (14 ASCII bytes including the trailing newline)

  2. /home/user/alerts/memory.log
     MEM: ok\n
     (8 ASCII bytes including the trailing newline)

  3. /home/user/alerts/disk.log
     DISK: 70%\n
     (10 ASCII bytes including the trailing newline)

  Expected state after successful task execution:

  Files/directories CREATED by the agent:
  • /home/user/alerts_archive.tar.gz
      – GNU tar archive, gzip compressed
      – Contains exactly three members at archive root:
            cpu.log (size 14 bytes)
            disk.log (size 10 bytes)
            memory.log (size 8 bytes)

  • /home/user/alerts_restore/          (directory)
      ├── cpu.log        (content identical to original, 14 bytes)
      ├── disk.log       (content identical to original, 10 bytes)
      ├── memory.log     (content identical to original, 8 bytes)
      └── restore_summary.log (33 bytes: 3 lines each terminated with “\n”)

  Exact content of /home/user/alerts_restore/restore_summary.log:
      cpu.log:14\n
      disk.log:10\n
      memory.log:8\n
  (Total 33 ASCII bytes.)

  No files other than the four listed above may exist inside /home/user/alerts_restore.

  The original directory /home/user/alerts and its three log files must remain unchanged.

  Automated tests will:
  • Verify that /home/user/alerts_archive.tar.gz exists and is readable.
  • List the archive and check for the exact three member names and sizes.
  • Verify that /home/user/alerts_restore exists.
  • Compute sha256sum of each original and restored log file: they must match.
  • Read /home/user/alerts_restore/restore_summary.log and compare byte-for-byte to the expected 33-byte string above.
name: task_000000_aed334eb
